structured.summary: The paper introduces AmbiBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate how both humans and language models handle task ambiguity, demonstrating that large models trained with reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) can perform comparably or better than humans in resolving ambiguously-specified tasks.

strengths.point: Introduction of a novel benchmark, AmbiBench, to study task ambiguity.
strengths.evidence: Section 1 Introduction and Section 3 The AmbiBench Benchmark.

strengths.point: Demonstrates that large models with RLHF can approach or exceed human performance in ambiguous tasks.
Provides a method to improve standard language models' performance on ambiguous tasks through finetuning.
strengths.evidence: Abstract and Section 3.3 In-Context Learning Formats.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of the types of ambiguity beyond binary classification tasks.
The benchmark may not fully capture real-world task complexity and ambiguity.
The paper does not provide a detailed analysis of why RLHF models outperform standard models.
Potential over-reliance on synthetic data which may not generalize to naturalistic settings.
The impact of different types of instructions on model performance is not deeply analyzed.

questions.question: How does the performance of models on AmbiBench compare to their performance on real-world ambiguous tasks?
questions.motivation: Understanding the generalizability of the benchmark to real-world applications is crucial for its utility.

questions.question: What specific features of RLHF contribute to its effectiveness in resolving task ambiguity?
questions.motivation: Identifying these features could help improve standard models without RLHF.

questions.question: How do different types of task ambiguity (beyond binary classification) affect model performance?
questions.motivation: Exploring a broader range of ambiguities could provide deeper insights into model capabilities.

critique_points.point_001: Limited exploration of the types of ambiguity beyond binary classification tasks. (Section 3.1 Selection of Tasks.)
critique_points.point_002: The benchmark may not fully capture real-world task complexity and ambiguity. (Section 3 Task Construction.)
critique_points.point_003: The paper does not provide a detailed analysis of why RLHF models outperform standard models. (Section 4.4 Discussion.)
critique_points.point_004: Potential over-reliance on synthetic data which may not generalize to naturalistic settings. (Section 3.2 Task Construction.)
critique_points.point_005: The impact of different types of instructions on model performance is not deeply analyzed. (Section 3.3 In-Context Learning Formats.)
critique_points.point_006: How does the performance of models on AmbiBench compare to their performance on real-world ambiguous tasks? (Understanding the generalizability of the benchmark to real-world applications is crucial for its utility.)
critique_points.point_007: What specific features of RLHF contribute to its effectiveness in resolving task ambiguity? (Identifying these features could help improve standard models without RLHF.)
critique_points.point_008: How do different types of task ambiguity (beyond binary classification) affect model performance? (Exploring a broader range of ambiguities could provide deeper insights into model capabilities.)
